content.text: Azure provides official, open-source SDKs for all major programming languages. Each SDK wraps the underlying Azure REST API in idiomatic, strongly-typed code for its language, handling authentication, pagination, retries, and error handling automatically.

Rather than constructing raw HTTP requests and managing bearer tokens manually, you import a library, create a client, and call methods that look like regular function calls. The SDK translates those calls to REST API requests, parses the JSON responses, and returns typed objects.

attrs.alt: Azure SDK architecture: language packages share a unified credential chain and REST transport layer

content.text: The Azure SDK team follows a set of design guidelines — called the Azure SDK Guidelines — to ensure consistency across all language SDKs. This means that once you learn the patterns in one language, the same concepts transfer directly to others.
DefaultAzureCredential works the same way in Python, JS, .NET, Go, and Java
Python asyncio, JavaScript Promises, .NET async/await all natively supported
Async Azure operations return a Poller object; call poller.result() to wait
List operations return iterators/async iterators that auto-fetch next pages
Every client accepts a logging option for request/response tracing
The Python Azure SDK is split into service-specific packages, all under the azure namespace on PyPI. The azure-identity package provides credential classes shared by all service packages.

# Install common packages
pip install azure-identity azure-mgmt-resource azure-mgmt-compute azure-mgmt-storage

# For data plane (e.g., reading blobs, sending messages):
pip install azure-storage-blob azure-keyvault-secrets azure-servicebus

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.compute.models import VirtualMachine, HardwareProfile, StorageProfile

# DefaultAzureCredential tries: env vars, workload identity, managed identity,
# Azure CLI, VS Code, Azure PowerShell — in that order.
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
subscription_id = 'your-subscription-id'

# Management plane client: controls Azure resources
resource_client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

# Create a resource group
rg = resource_client.resource_groups.create_or_update(
    'myRG',
    {'location': 'eastus', 'tags': {'env': 'prod'}}
)
print(f'Resource group: {rg.name} ({rg.location})')

# List all resource groups (auto-paginated iterator)
for group in resource_client.resource_groups.list():
    print(group.name, group.location)

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# Blob Storage: data plane client (no subscription ID needed — uses account URL)
blob_service = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url='https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net',
    credential=credential
)
container = blob_service.get_container_client('mycontainer')

# Upload a blob
with open('data.csv', 'rb') as f:
    container.upload_blob(name='data.csv', data=f, overwrite=True)

# List blobs
for blob in container.list_blobs():
    print(blob.name, blob.size)

# Key Vault: read a secret
kv_client = SecretClient(
    vault_url='https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net',
    credential=credential
)
secret = kv_client.get_secret('my-secret-name')
print(secret.value)

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
compute_client = ComputeManagementClient(credential, 'your-subscription-id')

# Start a VM (long-running operation — returns a Poller)
poller = compute_client.virtual_machines.begin_start('myRG', 'myVM')

# Option 1: block until done
result = poller.result()  # raises exception on failure
print('VM started')

# Option 2: poll manually
import time
while not poller.done():
    print(f'Status: {poller.status()}')
    time.sleep(5)
print('Done:', poller.result())
The JavaScript SDK is distributed as @azure/* scoped packages on npm. The same packages work in Node.js and in browsers (where applicable). TypeScript definitions are included.
The .NET Azure SDK packages follow the Azure.* prefix convention and are distributed on NuGet. They support async/await natively and return strongly typed models.

# Add packages via dotnet CLI
dotnet add package Azure.Identity
dotnet add package Azure.ResourceManager
dotnet add package Azure.ResourceManager.Compute
dotnet add package Azure.Storage.Blobs

DefaultAzureCredential is the recommended credential class across all Azure SDKs. It tries a chain of credential providers in order, using the first one that succeeds. This lets the same code work in local development, CI/CD, and production without code changes.
Running on AKS with workload identity federation enabled
Running on Azure VM, App Service, Container Apps, etc. with managed identity
Developer machine with az login session active
Developer machine with Connect-AzAccount session active
VS Code with Azure Account extension signed in
Falls back to browser login if all others fail (Python only by default)

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential, ClientSecretCredential

# In most cases — use DefaultAzureCredential
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# If you want to exclude certain credentials (e.g., no browser popup in CI):
credential = DefaultAzureCredential(
    exclude_interactive_browser_credential=True,
    exclude_visual_studio_code_credential=True
)

# Force managed identity explicitly (no fallback)
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()  # system-assigned
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id='your-user-assigned-mi-client-id')

# Service principal with secret (for CI/CD)
import os
credential = ClientSecretCredential(
    tenant_id=os.environ['AZURE_TENANT_ID'],
    client_id=os.environ['AZURE_CLIENT_ID'],
    client_secret=os.environ['AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET']
)

Never hard-code credentials in source code. Use DefaultAzureCredential in production (which picks up managed identity automatically) and environment variables or az login in local development. The same code works in both environments without modification.

Azure SDKs operate at two levels: the management plane (creating and configuring Azure resources through ARM) and the data plane (reading and writing data within those resources). Each level uses different service endpoints and may require different RBAC roles.
Upload a file to a container in that account
Storage Blob Data Contributor, Key Vault Secrets User, ...

All Azure SDK clients raise a consistent exception type on API errors. In Python it is HttpResponseError, in .NET it is RequestFailedException, and in JavaScript it is RestError. Always catch these to handle 404s, 409 conflicts, and 429 throttling.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.core.exceptions import HttpResponseError, ResourceNotFoundError

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, 'your-subscription-id')

try:
    rg = client.resource_groups.get('nonexistent-rg')
except ResourceNotFoundError:
    print('Resource group does not exist')
except HttpResponseError as e:
    print(f'API error {e.status_code}: {e.error.code} — {e.error.message}')
    if e.status_code == 429:
        retry_after = e.response.headers.get('Retry-After', '10')
        print(f'Rate limited. Retry after {retry_after}s')
Azure has over 200 service packages across each language. The Azure SDK release page (https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/) lists all packages with their stability status (GA, beta, or deprecated).

# Python: search for Azure SDK packages
pip index versions azure-mgmt-compute 2>/dev/null | head -5
pip show azure-mgmt-compute  # see installed version

# JavaScript: search npm for Azure SDK packages
npm info @azure/arm-compute version
npm info @azure/storage-blob version

# .NET: search NuGet
dotnet add package Azure.ResourceManager.Compute
dotnet list package | grep Azure

The Azure SDK for Go uses Go modules and follows the same credential and client patterns as other languages. Package names follow the pattern github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-go/sdk/resourcemanager/<service>/<package>.

# Go module initialization
go mod init myapp

# Add Azure SDK dependencies
go get github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-go/sdk/azidentity
go get github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-go/sdk/resourcemanager/resources/armresources
go get github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-go/sdk/resourcemanager/compute/armcompute
go get github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-go/sdk/storage/azblob
Testing Code That Uses Azure SDKs
SDK clients are interface-based in most languages, making them straightforward to mock in unit tests. This lets you test your business logic without making real Azure API calls.
Every Azure SDK client can emit detailed HTTP request/response logs. Enabling logging helps diagnose authentication failures, unexpected API responses, and throttling issues without guesswork.

import logging
import sys
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

# Enable Azure SDK HTTP logging
logging.basicConfig(stream=sys.stdout, level=logging.DEBUG)
azure_logger = logging.getLogger('azure')
azure_logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

# Now SDK calls print:
# - The HTTP method and URL
# - Request headers (Authorization: Bearer <redacted>)
# - Response status code and headers
# - Response body (if not binary)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, 'your-subscription-id')
rg = client.resource_groups.get('myRG')

# To log only errors (production setting):
azure_logger.setLevel(logging.WARNING)

Azure SDK clients include built-in retry logic for transient failures (network timeouts, 503 Service Unavailable). The default policy retries up to 3 times with exponential backoff. You can customize this per client.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.core.pipeline.policies import RetryPolicy

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# Custom retry policy: up to 5 retries, 2s initial delay
retry_policy = RetryPolicy(
    retry_total=5,
    retry_backoff_factor=2,     # 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 32s
    retry_on_status_codes={429, 503, 504}
)

client = ResourceManagementClient(
    credential,
    'your-subscription-id',
    retry_policy=retry_policy
)

# The client automatically retries 429 (rate limit) and 503 (service unavailable)
# For 429 responses, the SDK respects the Retry-After header automatically

The Azure SDK's EnvironmentCredential reads well-known environment variables for service principal authentication. Setting these variables is the standard pattern in CI/CD pipelines and container workloads.
Service principal / app registration client ID
Target subscription ID (used by your code, not the SDK auth)

# Set environment variables for a CI/CD pipeline
export AZURE_TENANT_ID='your-tenant-id'
export AZURE_CLIENT_ID='your-sp-client-id'
export AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET='your-sp-secret'
export AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID='your-subscription-id'

# Python: DefaultAzureCredential picks up these vars automatically
python3 -c "
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
import os
c = DefaultAzureCredential()
r = ResourceManagementClient(c, os.environ['AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID'])
print([rg.name for rg in r.resource_groups.list()][:3])
"

# GitHub Actions: use azure/login action to set these vars
# The action writes AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET
# (or OIDC federation tokens) to the workflow environment automatically.
Choosing Between SDK, CLI, and REST API
Each access method has strengths. The right choice depends on your use case.
Type safety, retry, pagination, auth chain built in
Concise, already in most pipeline images, no SDK dependency
Direct, no abstraction, shows exact JSON
Fastest to type, good defaults, -o table output
Preview features may not be in stable SDK yet

Azure SDKs wrap the REST API in idiomatic, language-native code for Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, C#/.NET, Go, Java, and more. All SDKs use the same DefaultAzureCredential authentication chain — which automatically selects between environment variables, workload identity, managed identity, and developer CLI credentials — so the same code works locally and in production without modification. SDK packages are split by service and by plane: management-plane packages (azure-mgmt-*, @azure/arm-*, Azure.ResourceManager.*) create and configure Azure resources; data-plane packages (azure-storage-blob, @azure/keyvault-secrets, Azure.Storage.Blobs, etc.) read and write data within those resources. Long-running operations return Poller objects; list operations return auto-paginated iterators. All SDK error types (HttpResponseError, RestError, RequestFailedException) expose the HTTP status code and ARM error code for structured exception handling.
